title: Personality Rights and Phone Tapping
focus: Discusses personality rights, phone tapping safeguards, and the constitutional validity of narco-analysis tests.
rationale: Fallback section derived from packet-level grouping.
sectionExcerpt:
[Page 028]
3.2. PERSONALITY RIGHTS
Why in the news?
Recently, Delhi High Court protected Personality Rights of Sadhguru Jaggi Vasudev from being misused via AI by websites and platforms.
About Personality Rights
- Definition: Right of an individual to control unauthorized use of their personal attributes like Name, Image, voice, Likeness and Distinctive expressions or traits.
- These rights include both commercial and non-commercial aspects.
- Not expressly mentioned in any statute in India.
- Components of Personality Rights:
- Right to publicity: Right to protect one’s image and likeness from being commercially exploited without permission.
- Partially and indirectly governed by statutes like Trademarks Act, 1999 and Copyright Act, 1957.
- Right to privacy: Right to not have one’s personality represented publicly without permission.
- Governed under Article 21 and through Supreme Court judgment in Justice K.S. Puttaswamy (Retd.) Case (2017).
- Posthumous Personality Rights in India: No specific statutory recognition protecting Posthumous Personality Rights.
- However, Emblems Act, 1950 prohibits commercial use of images of Mahatma Gandhi and Prime Minister.
- Deepa Jayakumar v. AL Vijay (2019) Judgment: Personality right, reputation or privacy enjoyed by a person during his lifetime comes to an end after his or her lifetime.
Judicial pronouncements on Personality rights in India
- ICC Development (International) Ltd. vs. Arvee Enterprises, 2003 (Delhi HC): Any effort to take away publicity right from individuals would be violative of Articles 19 and 21.
- Arun Jaitley vs Network Solutions Private Limited and Ors case, 2011 (Delhi HC): Popularity or fame of an individual will be no different on internet than in reality.
- Rajinikanth v. Varsha Productions (Madras HC, 2015): Using a celebrity’s name, image, or style without consent violates personality rights.
3.3. PHONE TAPPING
Why in the news?
Recently, Madras High Court has held that an individual's phone cannot be tapped in a secret operation to detect the commission of a crime, and the same would violate the individual's fundamental right to privacy.
